contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: A simple expand-or-shrink sum window is legal only when value constraints make sum movement monotonic; negative values can invalidate the discard rule and require prefix-based state or a stronger ordered structure.

items.prompt: Why can a negative value invalidate the rule "if the current sum is too large, move left rightward"?

options.text: Removing a negative left value can increase the sum instead of decreasing it.
Negative values cannot belong to contiguous intervals.
Prefix sums cannot contain negative totals.
A sliding window never moves its left boundary.

feedback.reason: The simple shrink rule assumes removing an element changes the aggregate in a predictable direction.
feedback.details: With a negative outgoing value, shrinking can make the sum larger, so discarded intervals may still contain valid answers.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_never_moves: Variable windows depend on left movement.

items.prompt: Why can a negative incoming value invalidate the rule "expand right to increase the sum"?

options.text: Adding the new value can decrease the current sum.
The right boundary begins moving backward.
Negative values make the array unsorted.
Sums containing negatives are undefined.

feedback.reason: Monotone window logic needs expansion and contraction to have known directional effects.
feedback.details: Negative values break that relationship even though boundaries still move forward.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_moves_left: The boundary can still move right; the aggregate direction is the problem.

items.prompt: The array is [4, -5, 6] and the target is an exact sum of 5. Which contiguous interval is a counterexample to discarding after the prefix sum drops?

options.text: [4] sums to 5.
[6] sums to 5.
[-5, 6] sums to 5.

feedback.reason: A temporary decrease does not mean a later extension cannot reach the exact target.
feedback.details: This demonstrates why local sum direction is not a safe discard signal with negative values.

items.prompt: A task asks whether any contiguous subarray has exact sum k, and values may be negative. Which state is generally appropriate?

options.text: Track cumulative sums and test whether currentPrefix - k occurred earlier.
Use the rule shrink when sum > k and expand when sum < k.
Sort values and use two pointers.
Assume negative values cancel harmlessly.

feedback.reason: A subarray from j to i has sum k exactly when prefix[i + 1] - prefix[j] = k.
feedback.details: A hash lookup for prefix[i + 1] - k does not rely on monotone value signs.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.simple_window: Neither comparison gives a legal direction when signs are mixed.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.positive_assumption: Their placement determines every interval sum.

items.prompt: The objective is the shortest subarray with sum at least k, and negative values are allowed. Which conclusion is correct?

options.text: A simple two-boundary sum window is not justified; a prefix-based structure with stronger ordering may be needed.
The ordinary positive-value shrink rule remains correct.
A frequency hash map alone always gives the shortest threshold interval.
Sort the array and use adjacent values.

feedback.reason: Mixed signs destroy the simple threshold window's discard proof.
feedback.details: A known solution uses prefix sums with a monotonic deque, illustrating that prefix state may need an additional ordered invariant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_positive_rule: Removing or adding values no longer changes the sum monotonically.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_hash_always: Shortest-at-least queries require order-aware handling of prefix values and indices.

items.prompt: All values are strictly positive. Which property restores the simple threshold-window rule?

options.text: Extending right increases the sum, and removing left decreases it.
All prefix sums become identical.
Index values become sorted.
A positive target alone is sufficient even with negative inputs.

feedback.reason: Positive values create a one-direction aggregate response to each boundary move.
feedback.details: That lets the algorithm prove when smaller or larger windows can be discarded.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_unique: Positive values make prefixes strictly increasing, not identical.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_positive: The input sign constraint is decisive.

items.prompt: All values are nonnegative and zeros are allowed. What changes for a threshold window?

options.text: Expansion is nondecreasing and shrinking is nonincreasing, so monotonicity remains but equal-sum plateaus need careful loop conditions.
Any zero breaks sliding-window correctness.
Every move changes the sum strictly.
Prefix hashing becomes the only legal method.

feedback.reason: Zeros preserve directional monotonicity but remove strict progress in the aggregate value.
feedback.details: Boundary indices must still move, and answer timing must handle equal-sum intervals correctly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fully_broken: Zero does not reverse sum direction.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.strict_only: Adding or removing zero can leave it unchanged.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_required: A window can still be valid for suitable threshold objectives.

items.prompt: A developer says, "The right pointer never moves backward, so the mixed-sign window is O(n) and therefore correct." What is wrong?

options.text: Forward-only pointers may bound work, but they do not prove that discarded intervals cannot contain the answer.
Two forward pointers always imply O(n²).
Negative values force the pointer to move backward in every algorithm.
Any O(n) algorithm returns the same answer.

feedback.reason: A runtime bound and a discard invariant are different proofs.
feedback.details: The algorithm can be fast and wrong if sign changes invalidate the boundary decision.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pointers_quadratic: They may be linear, but correctness is separate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.big_o_answer: Complexity says nothing about semantic correctness.

items.prompt: For exact sum k with arbitrary integers, what must a prefix-frequency map store to count all matching subarrays?

options.text: How many times each earlier prefix value has occurred.
Only the current window sum.
The values sorted ascending.
Only how often k appears as an element.

feedback.reason: Every earlier occurrence of currentPrefix - k defines a different starting boundary.
feedback.details: Frequency, not only existence, is required when the result is a count.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_current_sum: Different earlier boundaries can share the needed prefix value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_count: Subarray sums are not element equality.

items.prompt: Which test most directly exposes a false simple-window implementation for exact sums?

options.text: A prefix that overshoots or undershoots and later reaches the target after a negative or positive correction.
Only an array of ones.
Only empty input.
Only ascending positive values.

feedback.reason: A counterexample must force the aggregate direction to reverse after a boundary decision.
feedback.details: Mixed-sign recovery cases distinguish a memorized window template from a valid invariant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_ones: That satisfies monotonic assumptions and may hide the defect.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty_only: It does not exercise the discard rule.

items.prompt: Which facts explain why negative values break a simple sum window? Select all that apply.

options.text: Adding the next value can decrease the sum.
Removing the left value can increase the sum.
A comparison with the target may no longer prove which boundary to move.
Negative values make the selected interval noncontiguous.

feedback.reason: Mixed signs break directional aggregate reasoning, not the definition of an interval.
feedback.details: The algorithm needs another state relation that does not depend on sign monotonicity.

items.prompt: Which objectives remain natural for prefix-based reasoning with arbitrary signed values? Select all that apply.

options.text: Existence of a subarray with exact sum k.
Count of subarrays with exact sum k.
Longest subarray with exact sum k.
Shortest subarray with sum at least k using only shrink-when-valid logic.

feedback.reason: Prefix differences express exact subarray sums without sign assumptions.
feedback.details: Threshold optimization may require additional order-aware prefix structures.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.simple_threshold_window: Mixed signs invalidate that simple rule.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_exists: Look for an earlier prefix equal to currentPrefix - k.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.longest_exact: Store the earliest index for each prefix.

items.prompt: Which input constraints support the ordinary variable threshold window? Select all that apply.

options.text: All elements are strictly positive.
All elements are nonnegative, with loop rules handling zeros.
Elements may be positive or negative with no stronger invariant.
The target is positive but values are arbitrary.

feedback.reason: The value domain, not merely the target, supplies the monotone boundary.
feedback.details: Zeros require careful equality handling but do not reverse direction.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_signed: The simple sum comparison is not a safe discard rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_only: Target sign does not restore monotonicity.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.positive: Boundary moves change sum in strict directions.

items.prompt: Which are valid review tests for sign-sensitive window logic? Select all that apply.

options.text: A negative value leaves the window, causing the sum to increase.
A negative value enters, causing the sum to decrease.
Long runs of zeros around a threshold.
Only one positive example whose first window is already valid.

feedback.reason: Boundary tests must exercise the exact assumptions used by the discard rule.
feedback.details: A convenient positive hit can pass even when mixed-sign logic is wrong.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.positive_only_mid: This does not test the boundary failure.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.zeros: This tests non-strict monotonicity and pointer progress.

items.prompt: Which mistaken claims specifically hide the sign assumption in window reasoning? Select all that apply.

options.text: Every contiguous-sum problem is a sliding-window problem.
Forward-only pointers prove correctness.
Negative values only change arithmetic, not boundary legality.
A subarray sum equals a difference of two prefix sums.

feedback.reason: These heuristics erase the invariant that makes boundary movement safe.
feedback.details: The prefix-difference identity remains valid for all numeric signs.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous_implies_window: Prefix methods also target contiguous sums, and sign/objective constraints matter.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.forward_implies_correct: They address work, not safe elimination.

items.prompt: Arrange the proof audit for a proposed mixed-sign sliding window.

elements.text: State the exact condition that moves left or right.
Ask how adding or removing a negative value changes the maintained sum.
Construct an interval discarded by the rule that can later become valid.
Use prefix-based or stronger ordered state when the rule is not monotone.

feedback.reason: A correct audit attacks the discard implication directly.
feedback.details: Finding a directional reversal proves that the simple template is unsupported.

items.prompt: Arrange the exact-sum prefix-hash workflow for arbitrary integers.

elements.text: Store prefix value 0 before reading elements.
Add the current value to the running prefix.
Query whether or how often runningPrefix - k occurred earlier.
Store the current prefix according to the result contract.

feedback.reason: The zero prefix permits intervals that begin at index 0.
feedback.details: Looking up before storing avoids counting a zero-length interval unless the contract explicitly permits it.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning that restores a simple threshold window under nonnegative values.

elements.text: Establish that every value is nonnegative.
Show extending right cannot decrease the sum.
Show removing left cannot increase the sum.
Use those implications to prove which intervals can be skipped.

feedback.reason: The algorithm follows from directional implications, not from the word subarray.
feedback.details: Zeros make the implications non-strict but preserve them.

items.prompt: Count all exact-sum-k subarrays in n arbitrary signed values using a running prefix and a hash-frequency map. Select expected time and auxiliary space.
constraints: Expected hash lookup and update are O(1).
Up to n + 1 distinct prefix values may be stored.

feedback.reason: Each element contributes one prefix update and one expected lookup.
feedback.details: The method replaces invalid monotone pointer logic with retained cumulative-state history.

items.prompt: A naive algorithm checks every contiguous subarray sum by extending from each start index in an n-value array. Select worst-case time and auxiliary space.
constraints: Each extension updates the sum in O(1).
No table of all sums is stored.

feedback.reason: There are Θ(n²) contiguous start-end pairs.
feedback.details: The comparison highlights why prefix-hash state is valuable when a simple O(n) window is invalid.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: A simple expand-or-shrink sum window is legal only when value constraints make sum movement monotonic; negative values can invalidate the discard rule and require prefix-based state or a stronger ordered structure.
